title: Nonlinear Hall quantum oscillations to probe topological Brown-Zak fermions in graphene moiré systems
authors: Jinrui Zhong, Huimin Peng, Yuqing Hu, Qi Feng, Qiuli Li, Shihao Zhang, Qinsheng Wang, Jinhai Mao, Junxi Duan, Yugui Yao
abstract: Due to the deep connection with the quantum geometry of electronic Bloch wavefunctions, the second-order nonlinear Hall effect (NLHE) has been an attractive topic since its proposal. However, studies on NLHE under a magnetic field have been lacking. Given that quantum oscillations in the linear response regime have been proven to be useful tools in investigating electronic systems, searching for quantum oscillations in NLHE is of great interest and is expected to provide new avenues to unveil rich quantum geometric properties of novel quasiparticles. Here, we propose a new type of NLHE quantum oscillations and experimentally probe it in graphene moiré systems. It stems from the alternation of the dominant NLHE mechanisms with recurring Bloch states under magnetic field, which enables sensitive detection of Brown-Zak fermions, giving an onset field as low as 0.5 T. Most importantly, when the commensurability condition is satisfied, the nonlinear transport of Brown-Zak fermions is mainly governed by quantum geometric contributions. Our findings not only establish a new type of quantum oscillations, but also demonstrate the first experimental detection of the topological nature of Brown-Zak fermions, shedding light on the exploration of novel topological quasiparticles.
summary: The researchers identify a new type of quantum oscillation in the nonlinear Hall effect of graphene moiré systems. These oscillations allow for the sensitive detection of Brown-Zak fermions at much lower magnetic fields than traditional methods. Crucially, the study proves that the nonlinear transport at the commensurability point is driven by quantum geometric properties.
main_problem: The difficulty in detecting the quantum geometric properties and topological nature of Brown-Zak fermions in the linear response regime due to the dominance of Landau levels and high magnetic field requirements.
main_result: The discovery of Nonlinear Hall Brown-Zak Oscillations (NHBZO) and the first experimental demonstration that the nonlinear transport of Brown-Zak fermions is primarily governed by quantum geometric contributions.
method: Second-harmonic transport measurements in twisted double bilayer graphene, utilizing scaling analysis of temperature-dependent conductivity and angle-dependent magnetic field sweeps.
model_or_system: Twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) moiré superlattices with a twist angle greater than 2.5 degrees, operated in a regime where electronic correlations are minimized to focus on quantum geometry.
key_observables: Second-order nonlinear Hall voltage (V_y,2w), longitudinal resistance (R_xx), Hall resistivity (rho_xy), and the differential nonlinear Hall signal (dV_y,2w/dB).
important_parameters: Magnetic field (B) and tilt angle (theta), carrier density (n), displacement field (D), temperature (T), and driving current (I_omega).
main_assumptions: The twist angle is large enough to minimize electronic correlation effects, allowing the focus to remain on quantum geometric properties.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows TDBG characterization and the observation of nonlinear Hall oscillations; Fig 2 maps the oscillations in the (n, 1/B) plane; Fig 3 demonstrates the orbital nature and D-independence of the peaks; Fig 4 presents the temperature-dependent scaling analysis of the nonlinear Hall conductivity.
paper_structure: The paper begins with device characterization and confirmation of nonlinear transport, moves to the identification of new quantum oscillations (NHBZO), proves their orbital and commensurability-driven nature, and concludes with a scaling analysis to isolate quantum geometric contributions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
